text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 20 December 2020 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

Alex Morgan, DOB: 22-07-1989, Hospital No: U918273 NHS No. 3029471156  
Flat 4, 28 Willow Street, London, N1 7JG 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Epilepsy with prior adherence difficulties now supported by smartphone alarm and NFC tag reminders; history of intermittent mood disturbance under GP review; vitamin D insufficiency under replacement; lives independently with community support. 

Medication: Levetiracetam 1500 mg twice daily.  
Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily.  
Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily, Omeprazole 20 mg daily.  

I reviewed Alex in clinic today. He attended alone but brought a written log and showed me his smartphone reminder setup. He has implemented multiple daily alarms alongside small NFC tags placed by his medication box and bedside table which he taps to confirm taken doses; this follows previous missed evening doses earlier this year. He reports the system has markedly improved consistency over the past six weeks, with only one late evening dose and no fully missed doses recorded since starting the NFC tags. Sleep has been irregular due to shift work changes, and he notes occasional early morning grogginess but denies daytime sedation. Appetite is stable. No intercurrent illness reported. 

Seizures: Alex described two nocturnal clusters this month; each ~four - five events, occurring during sleep on separate weeks. He reports brief arousals with confusion and tongue soreness on the following mornings, without clear daytime events. No injuries, no incontinence, and no prolonged post-event weakness. He has not required rescue medication and has not presented to ED. He is keeping a diary and has begun linking events to nights of reduced sleep. 

Examination: Alert and oriented, speech fluent. Cranial nerves intact. Power 5/5 throughout, tone normal, reflexes symmetrical, coordination intact, gait normal. No tongue lacerations today; mild lateral bite marks healing. 

Investigations: Last bloods (U&Es, LFTs, FBC) 3 months ago were within reference ranges; vitamin D previously low and now on replacement—repeat level requested via GP. Prior MRI brain and routine EEG on file without definitive lateralising findings. 

Plan:  
- Continue current anti-seizure medications unchanged.  
- Reinforce ongoing use of smartphone alarms and NFC tag confirmations given improved adherence.  
- Sleep hygiene discussed; he will continue diary correlation between sleep duration and nocturnal events.  
- Safety advice reviewed for nocturnal events (pillow positioning, bed environment, and alarm alert for a trusted contact—patient to consider non-urgent).  
- Request GP to recheck vitamin D level and general bloods in 8 weeks.  
- Arrange follow-up in 4 months; earlier review if clusters increase, events generalise, or any injury/prolonged confusion occurs.  

Yours sincerely

entities.text: two nocturnal clusters this month; each ~four - five events

attributes.Label: 2 cluster per month, 4 to 5 per cluster

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly describes seizure activity: "two nocturnal clusters this month; each ~four - five events, occurring during sleep on separate weeks." This provides both the cluster frequency and the number of events per cluster. The timeframe is the current month, indicating a rate of 2 clusters per month, with 4 to 5 seizures per cluster. There is no indication of complete seizure freedom for 6 months; thus we must report the described frequency. No other frequencies are given that would supersede this.
